data.title: Rightmove's Forecast-Cut Points to Challenges Ahead — Market Talk
data.description: Rightmove's downgrade to its house-price forecast, following record sales last year, points to a more challenging second half, Begbies Traynor analyst Julie Palmer says. This is due particularly to higher stock levels and affordability concerns weighing on buyer sentiment, the analyst adds in a note. The property portal's move into housing-adjacent markets like mortgages and rentals is bearing fruit, Palmer says. Still, rivals are looking to capitalize and agents are facing pressure on margins. Hence, the coming months will test its ability to adapt and defend its leading position, Palmer adds. "While falling mortgage rates and looser lending criteria may support activity, political and economic uncertainty could dampen any recovery," she says. Shares are down 1.6% at 782.60 pence and are up 38% over the past 12 months.(anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0927 GMT - Rightmove's downgrade to its house-price forecast, following record sales last year, points to a more challenging second half, Begbies Traynor analyst Julie Palmer says. This is due particularly to higher stock levels and affordability concerns weighing on buyer sentiment, the analyst adds in a note. The property portal's move into housing-adjacent markets like mortgages and rentals is bearing fruit, Palmer says. Still, rivals are looking to capitalize and agents are facing pressure on margins. Hence, the coming months will test its ability to adapt and defend its leading position, Palmer adds. "While falling mortgage rates and looser lending criteria may support activity, political and economic uncertainty could dampen any recovery," she says. Shares are down 1.6% at 782.60 pence and are up 38% over the past 12 months.(anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0914 GMT - Base metals have outperformed other commodities in the month to date on a weaker U.S. dollar and bullish sentiment, BMI analysts say in a note. LME three-month copper is up 1.6% on month at $9,848.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum is up 2.3% on month at $2,652.50 a ton. The weaker dollar has made it cheaper for international purchasers to buy dollar-denominated commodities, boosting demand. Market optimism has grown for mainland Chinese demand on pledges for new industrial investments. Copper prices were jolted by President Trump's larger-than-expected 50% tariff announcement, BMI says. That said, once tariffs kick in with a definitive announcement, LME copper prices are likely to slide as the rush to import the metal to the U.S. ends and availability elsewhere improves, BMI says. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0910 GMT - NatWest is well placed to weather a potential credit downturn given the resilience of its customers, the bank's finance chief tells CNBC. The bank's economic assumptions haven't moved materially despite all the noise, Katie Murray says. Impairment charges, which reflect bad loans and is a key metric for the group to see how financially healthy people are, are at historically low levels, she says. Signs of an economic deterioration aren't showing up in its numbers. "It's not something we see in the day to day activity of how people are behaving, the lending that people are taking out, or the money that they're depositing, or the investments we're seeing into shares," Murray adds. Shares are up 1.6% and 27% year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0905 GMT - The new midterm targets outlined by Sabadell at its investor day show that it is buckling up for growth, JB Capital says in a research note. This strengthens the view that suitor BBVA has to increase its offer price to secure meaningful acceptance, analysts write. They calculate that the offer can be enhanced by up to 48%. The Spanish bank's growth ambitions--driven by corporate and consumer loans as mortgages should align with the market--seem reasonable and cost of risk remains under control, they say. The lender's aim to add two to three percentage points to its 8% market share in the coming years seems realistic and in line with the country's nominal GDP growth expectations, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0900 GMT - Volkswagen's new guidance is already under pressure due to a profit warning from subsidiary Traton, especially at the free cash flow level, UBS analyst Patrick Hummel writes. The bank expects slight negative consensus revisions, mainly on free cash flow. Volkswagen now sees flat revenue on year from up to 5% growth previously, with an operating profit margin of 4%-5%. Free cash flow is now guided at 1 billion-3 billion euros. "However, the new guidance doesn't include last night's profit warning from Traton, which shaves off 1 billion euros free cash flow and around 500 million euros of operating profit at VW group level." Shares rise 2.6%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0845 GMT - China's pharmaceutical sector is likely to sustain its strong momentum, HSBC analysts say in a research note. The sector has outperformed the market year-to-date, with the Hang Seng Healthcare Index up more than 70%, driven by breakthroughs in business development such as 3S Bio's licensing deals with Pfizer, they note. A further sector re-rating likely after a robust 1H performance, they say. HSBC maintains buy ratings on leading players including Jiangsu Hengrui Pharmaceuticals, Sino Biopharmaceutical and Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0843 GMT - Eni delivered a good set of results, with net profit and earnings before interest and taxes beating expectations, Jefferies analysts Giacomo Romeo andKai Ye Loh write. This is mainly due to a strong performance in its gas and liquefied natural gas division and a lower general tax rate, they write. Investors will likely question Eni's management on the details of its joint venture with Petronas, sources of cost reductions and the timeline for final investment decisions on Friday's earnings call, they write. The call is scheduled for 12:00 GMT. Shares trade flat at 14.408 euros.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0839 GMT - Deutsche Bank's shares had a sharp rise on what was an average set of results and limited evidence of revenue progression in its corporate and private bank, Citi says in a research note. Citi cuts its rating on the German bank to sell from neutral. While reaction to the quarterly print seems to have been driven by commentary provided on regulatory risk-weighted asset inflation risk, this wasn't much of a concern for Citi analysts. Their fears are focused on whether Deutsche Bank will be able to meet its revenue targets and the higher bar for capital returns, they say. "The bank has become a 'concept stock' but after [an around] 60% re-rating, we would be very cautious on allocating more capital here, until there is clear evidence of earnings support flowing through," they say. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0838 GMT - Grab could gain market share in the delivery space through its new budget-friendly products, says DBS's Sachin Mittal in a note. The group is expected to see higher order frequency, as such products include lower-cost delivery options and are likely to expand the company's Southeast Asia customer base. Its current monthly active users in the delivery and ride-hailing businesses cover only about 6% of the population in the region. DBS raises its FY 2025 and FY 2026 forecasts for adjusted Ebitda by 1% and 4%, respectively, and maintains a buy call on the stock. It raises its target price to $6.35 from $5.68. Shares last traded at US$5.34. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0832 GMT - Diesel margins are expected to moderate from current levels but to remain above prepandemic averages due to persistent structural tightness in refining capacity, according to Goldman Sachs. Despite the normalization of crude-oil prices and lower geopolitical risks, margins continued to rise in July. This surge was driven by a 10%-15% drop in global diesel stocks year-over-year, unexpected refinery outages in Europe, and strong financial demand for diesel. Goldman Sachs forecasts diesel refining margins will remain $10 a barrel above their 2013-19 average through the second half of this year and into 2026. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0817 GMT - Eni is set for a strong second half of the year and 2026 as its divestment plan and self-help measures deliver results, RBC Capital Markets' Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. The Italian oil major's balance sheet is more resilient than it has been for many years and free cash flow is growing, they write. Eni reported a decent set of second-quarter results, with a strong performance in its global gas and LNG unit delivering a 5% beat to expectations for group earnings before interest and taxes, they add. Shares trade flat at 14.40 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0814 GMT - China's plan to rectify a price war in the pharma industry should bode well for the sector, DBS says in a note. In future selections of medical-products suppliers for public hospitals, the lowest bid might not win, China's National Healthcare Security Administration says. DBS thinks this move alleviates the largest risk to China's pharma sector--price cuts. Chinese pharma stocks could have further room to grow as the valuation isn't high from a historical perspective, it says. In view of better industry environment, the market could raise pharma companies' 2H sales and earnings forecasts, it adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Slovenia Tourist Arrivals Soar in June
data.description:
Tourist arrivals in Slovenia soared 17.2% year-on-year to 794,737 in June 2025, following a 3.5% rise in May.

The number of foreign tourist arrivals increased by 20.2% to 2.078 million while domestic arrivals grew 6.4% to 721,368.

Most foreign visitors came from Germany, Austria, Czech Republic, the Netherlands and Poland.

Meanwhile, overnight stays surged 19.5% to 1.970 million, led by stays in mountain resorts (35% of all overnight stays), seaside resorts (20%), health (spa) resorts and Ljubljana (15%).

Considering the first six months of the year, tourists generated 2.8 million arrivals (8% more than in the same period last year) and 6.9 million overnight stays (8% more).

Foreign tourists generated 71% of all overnight stays, the largest number tourists from Germany, followed by tourists from Italy and Austria.

data.title: Powell Under Fire As Housing Slows
data.description:
The 10?year Treasury yield slipped after President Trump took to Truth Social Wednesday to blast Fed Chair Jerome Too Late Powell for keeping rates at 4.25%4.50%, saying a 300 bps cut could save the U.S. $1 T a year.

He pointed to June's single?family home sales dropping to a 627,000 SAAR pace, down 6.6% Y/Y, unsold inventory swelling to a 9.8?month supply and 30?year mortgage rates hovering near 7%.

Traders jumped on his comments: the 2?year yield tumbled 7 bps, the 10?year eased 6 bps, the 2s/10s curve steepened by 60 bps, the dollar slid about 0.3% and the Russell 2000 popped 1%.

Any hint of political meddling in Fed policy could shake confidence in bonds and the dollar.Investors will be glued to Powell's September testimony for clues on when rate cuts might land.

data.title: Get Ready for New Rules on Tax Breaks for Charitable Giving — Tax Report — WSJ
data.description:
By Laura Saunders

Do you make charitable donations? Little-noticed provisions in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act are about to boost tax breaks for some donors, lower them for others and complicate them for lots of Americans.

Unlike some other recent changes, these don't take effect until 2026 — so donors who care about maximizing charitable tax breaks have months to prepare. After that, the changes are permanent, at least until Congress alters the law again.

To understand the new provisions, go back to the 2017 tax overhaul. It nearly doubled the standard deduction, which is the amount filers can deduct if they don't list itemized deductions on Schedule A. It also capped the deduction for state and local taxes, or SALT, at $10,000 per return.

Those changes pushed millions more filers into taking the standard deduction, which meant they could no longer list charitable donations on Schedule A and get a specific break for them. Between 2017 and 2019, itemized charitable deductions dropped by about $66 billion, or 26%, according to the Giving USA Foundation and Indiana University.

To encourage giving, the new tax law goes in a different direction. It allows donors who don't itemize on Schedule A to take annual deductions of as much as $2,000 for married joint filers and $1,000 for singles.

The new deduction is a much-expanded version of pandemic provisions from 2020 and 2021. It could allow charitable deductions for about 100 million people who wouldn't be eligible otherwise.

However, two other provisions will impose new limits on itemized charitable deductions. Donors who list donations on Schedule A won't get their full benefit, because they'll forgo an amount equal to 0.5% of their adjusted gross income. In addition, top-bracket taxpayers will only get to take itemized deductions at 35%, not 37%, which will affect charitable donations.

The upshot is that givers who want to maximize tax breaks should plan now.

"People need to decide whether to accelerate contributions into this year, make them on a normal schedule or defer them into next year," says Lawrence Katzenstein, a lawyer with Thompson Coburn in St. Louis who specializes in charitable tax planning.

Giving strategies will depend on individual circumstances. Donors might want to accelerate gifts into 2025 to avoid the new haircuts on itemized charitable deductions, especially if they're in the top tax bracket.

Others who will benefit from expanded SALT deductions that begin this year might want to clean out attics and closets to make property donations this year, in case they won't be itemizing in the future.

Still others who are 70 1/2 or older might want to give using qualified charitable distributions, or QCDs, from their traditional IRAs. These donations aren't subject to the new limits, and QCDs are often the most tax-advantaged way to make donations.

To help with planning, here are details of the new charitable provisions.

The $2,000 or $1,000 deduction

This tax break is for donors who don't itemize deductions on Schedule A. It's $2,000 annually for married joint filers and $1,000 for others.

There are several limits. The gifts must be of cash, so donations of property such as clothing or furniture don't count. Neither do gifts of assets such as stock. In addition, the gifts must be to qualified charities such as a church or school, not to donor-advised funds or supporting organizations.

Another limit: This deduction will reduce a filer's taxable income but not adjusted gross income, or AGI. This matters because many other provisions — including Medicare Irmaa premiums, the income limits for Roth IRA contributions and the 3.8% investment-income surtax — are based on AGI. To hold down AGI, filers need to look elsewhere, such as traditional IRA, 401(k) and HSA contributions, or QCDs, or business losses.

The 0.5% disallowance

This provision imposes a threshold on filers' itemized charitable contributions on Schedule A. For most, it disallows an amount of the donation that's equal to 0.5% of adjusted gross income.

For example, a couple who itemizes and has $225,000 of AGI couldn't deduct $1,125 of their charitable donations.

Note that the disallowance is a fixed amount for each year. So if this couple gives $2,000, their deduction will be $875. That means they will have lost more than half of their deduction. But if they give $5,000, their deduction will be $3,875. In that case, they'll lose just over 20%.

But wait. What if this couple opts not to itemize and claims the $2,000 charitable deduction for nonitemizers. Would they be better off?

Maybe not, if they're reaping big benefits from deductions for SALT, mortgage interest, and others. Instead, they might opt to bunch their charitable donations and make them every few years instead of annually to maximize their value.

The limit for top-bracket taxpayers

This provision clips the highest-income filers by allowing the benefit for itemized deductions only at 35% rather than the top rate of 37%. Currently the top bracket begins at roughly $752,000 for joint filers and $626,000 for single filers, and it will be higher next year when this limit takes effect, because of inflation adjustments.

Because top-bracket taxpayers' income will be too high for the expanded $40,000 SALT deduction, this change will especially affect charitable donations. It's in addition to the 0.5% disallowance.

Richard Pon, a CPA in San Francisco, illustrates the potential effect on a top-bracket, joint-filing couple who have $900,000 of AGI and plan to make a $100,000 donation. Under current law in effect until year-end, their $100,000 gift would yield tax savings of $37,000.

Under next year's law, the couple's tax savings could drop to $33,425, costing them an extra $3,575. That is because of the combined effect of the 0.5% disallowance and the limit on itemized deductions.

Donor-advised funds, or DAFs, could come in handy here. The giver could contribute a large amount to the DAF, get a full deduction for 2025 and then make smaller gifts from the account over time if desired.

Says Pon, "I expect to see accelerated charitable donations this year from the smartest people in the highest bracket, because of the double tax whammy."

Write to Laura Saunders at Laura.Saunders@wsj.com

data.title: Banks Are Getting Pickier About Whom They Want as Credit-Card Customers — WSJ
data.description:
By Imani Moise

Banks are making credit cards harder to get — for some customers.

Lenders opened fewer cards in the second quarter, according to earnings reports from major issuers. They raised qualification requirements for lower-end customers that tend to be at greater risk of missing payments.

It was a different story for higher-end customers, which are increasingly propelling the U.S. economy. JPMorgan Chase and Citigroup introduced revamped premium credit cards in recent weeks, and American Express teased a big update to its Platinum card this fall.

"The highest, fastest-growing part of our card business has been with the heavier spenders," Capital One Chief Executive Richard Fairbank told analysts on a call this week. The bank last month opened a new luxury lounge — complete with a cheesemonger station — in New York's John F. Kennedy International Airport for holders of its $395 a year Venture X card.

But more banks have raised the bar for credit-card approvals this year than lowered it, reflecting growing caution, according to the Federal Reserve's Senior Loan Officer Survey.

Total new credit-card openings across four major lenders fell 5% in the second quarter, marking the first decline in more than a year.

At American Express, new account openings fell 6% from a year earlier. But the average fee per card rose to $117 from $101 a year earlier, driven by stronger uptake of premium cards.

Lenders tend to market their cards heavily, but recently they have been targeting consumers with higher credit scores. In April, more than 87% of card-related mail volume was prescreened, meaning the offers were only sent to consumers who already had met certain credit criteria. That is the highest level in nearly three years, according to Competiscan, a marketing-analysis firm.

While the shift helps reduce risk, consumers with exceptional credit represent less than a quarter of the total credit-card market, said Megan Cipperly, vice president at Competiscan.

"Only a small subset of consumers are receiving the lion's share of credit-card offers — and they're not necessarily the ones who need more credit," she said.

Banks are eager to attract big spenders with high scores because they are lucrative and relatively low-risk. Every time a cardholder swipes, banks collect an interchange fee from the merchant. Such customers are also more likely to pay their monthly credit-card bills in full, so the risk of default is low.

Heavy spenders also can offset pullbacks in spending from more cautious consumers. At American Express, spending on airline tickets were flat overall, but spending on front-of-cabin seats rose 10% and short-term rental stays over $5,000 increased 9%.

Lower-end consumers, meanwhile, are struggling to keep up: Card balances are rising — a sign that many households are spending beyond their means. Those balances also have been getting more expensive this year. The average interest rate on credit cards rose to 24.35% this month, according to LendingTree.

So far, delinquencies have remained steady. The economy hasn't been derailed by trade tensions and growing concerns about the job market. But banks remain wary.

"The U.S. economy remained resilient in the quarter," said JPMorgan Chase chief Jamie Dimon, during earnings last week. But "significant risks persist."

Write to Imani Moise at imani.moise@wsj.com

data.title: Airlines Are Having a Bruising Year. Delta and United Are Doing Better. — WSJ
data.description:
By Alison Sider

For the airline industry, 2025 has been a tough year.

On Thursday, Southwest said about $1 billion in earnings before interest and taxes it had anticipated this year have been wiped away, while American forecast a third-quarter loss.

Many consumers sat out flying to their summer vacations this year as economic worries made price-sensitive travelers more cautious. Airlines have had to cut fares to fill seats, especially on domestic leisure routes.

"Let's face it, the domestic network has been under stress because of the uncertainty in the economy and the reluctance of domestic passengers to get in the game," American CEO Robert Isom said during an earnings call Thursday.

Airlines started the year on an optimistic note, betting that a voracious appetite for travel would continue, business travel would maintain its steady postpandemic climb, and customers would absorb higher fares.

Their hopes were quickly dashed. A series of safety incidents, including a fatal crash in January, set fliers on edge. Then questions about the trajectory of the economy began to swirl. As President Trump announced broad new tariffs in April, airlines yanked their guidance for the year, saying it was too hard to predict how it would all play out.

Airline executives now say they think the worst is behind them. Consumers and businesses are getting comfortable enough to start making plans again. And carriers are culling their schedules — eliminating an overhang of unfilled seats that outpaced demand and weighed on ticket prices.

While they are no longer predicting profits as robust as they had at the start of the year, United and Delta expressed confidence in the second half of 2025. American and Southwest also say they believe a turnaround is under way, but they have been hit harder because of their focus on U.S. flying, where demand has been weakest.

American CEO Isom said July would likely be the low point compared with the same month a year ago, with bookings increasing each month while demand improves and carriers cut back on adding seats.

Premium lift

One dynamic playing out in the industry is the power of wealthy travelers who continue to spend on premium seats and international trips.

American and Alaska Air Group, which also reported second-quarter results this week, cited resilient spending from higher-income consumers and strong revenue from premium cabins.

Delta and United, which have expansive global networks and aggressively appeal to more affluent customers, have dominated industry profits.

Delta Chief Executive Ed Bastian said his airline's target customers — households that earn at least $100,000 a year — haven't been pulling back, despite travelers' heartburn earlier this year over tariffs and the U.S. economy. "That cohort has accumulated a significant amount of wealth in the post-Covid era," Bastian said earlier this month.

The new Southwest

Southwest, however, is only just now starting to shift away from its one-size-fits-all product to tap in to what more upscale consumers want. Next week, Southwest will start selling tickets with assigned seats and extra legroom for flights next year — a first for the carrier.

Southwest CEO Bob Jordan said Thursday that revenue from newly adopted bag fees has exceeded the airline's expectations.

The airline's new bare-bones basic economy fares, however, had a rocky rollout. They were initially a drag on revenue when they launched in May, as customers didn't end up going through with purchases.

The airline said it has resolved those problems, and Jordan said he has been encouraged by the number of customers paying up for pricier tickets.

A few months ago, it was tough to fill planes, even with promotions, Jordan said. "We're starting to see the volume return. I think that's a strong sign it's a broad-based recovery," he said.

July jitters

While airline executives predict a better second half, some of the weakness could extend into the third quarter. Bookings have picked up, but American executives said that most people made their July travel plans in more uncertain times.

"July is going to be a really tough month," American Chief Financial Officer Devon May said in an interview. "All the bookings were effectively taken for July during this time when consumer sentiment was really low, and there was more uncertainty than there is today."

The recovery could be too late to salvage profits for some airlines.

JetBlue Airways CEO Joanna Geraghty told employees in a memo last month the airline's hopes of breaking even again this year were looking unlikely.

"We're hopeful demand and bookings will rebound, but even a recovery won't fully offset the ground we've lost this year, and our path back to profitability will take longer than we'd hoped," she wrote. "That means we're still relying on borrowed cash to keep the airline running."

Write to Alison Sider at alison.sider@wsj.com

data.title: Volkswagen reverses course after investor call post outlook cut
data.description:
** Shares in Volkswagen rise 3.6%, reversing course after falling around 3.5% in early trading, as management comments during an earnings call soothed some investor concerns

** "(The move) likely stems from encouraging remarks made by management during the earnings call, where they, for instance, indicated that Porsche and Audi performance is expected to hit a trough this year, which implies some recovery in 2026," says Metzler analyst Pal Skirta

** VW's CEO also told investors that the German automaker needed to speed up savings efforts after outlook cut

** The guidance cut was "much expected", Jefferies analysts say in a note, while pointing to resilience in VW's Q2 margin and those of its core volume brands

** The company forecast FY operating return on sales of 4-5%, down from 5.5-6.5% previously, and flat year-on-year sales vs earlier guidance of up to 5%

($1 = 0.8517 euros)

data.title: REG - Merloni Holding Dundee Precious Met. Adriatic Metals - Form 8.3 - Dundee Precious Metals Inc.
data.description:
RNS Number : 6260S Merloni Holding S.p.A 25 July 2025  

FORM 8.3

PUBLIC OPENING POSITION DISCLOSURE/DEALING DISCLOSURE BY

A PERSON WITH INTERESTS IN RELEVANT SECURITIES REPRESENTING 1% OR MORE

Rule 8.3 of the Takeover Code (the "Code")

1.         KEY INFORMATION

(a) Full name of discloser:

Merloni Holding S.p.A.

(b) Owner or controller of interests and short positions disclosed, if different from 1(a):

     The naming of nominee or vehicle companies is insufficient.  For a trust, the trustee(s), settlor and beneficiaries must be named.

Mr. Paolo Merloni

(c) Name of offeror/offeree in relation to whose relevant securities this form relates:

     Use a separate form for each offeror/offeree

Dundee Precious Metals Inc.

(d) If an exempt fund manager connected with an offeror/offeree, state this and specify identity of offeror/offeree:

(e) Date position held/dealing undertaken:

     For an opening position disclosure, state the latest practicable date prior to the disclosure

24 July 2025

(f)  In addition to the company in 1(c) above, is the discloser making disclosures in respect of any other party to the offer?

     If it is a cash offer or possible cash offer, state "N/A"

Yes, Adriatic Metals Plc.

2.         POSITIONS OF THE PERSON MAKING THE DISCLOSURE

If there are positions or rights to subscribe to disclose in more than one class of relevant securities of the offeror or offeree named in 1(c), copy table 2(a) or (b) (as appropriate) for each additional class of relevant security.

(a)        Interests and short positions in the relevant securities of the offeror or offeree to which the disclosure relates following the dealing (if any)

Class of relevant security:

Common

Interests

Short positions

Number

%

Number

%

(1) Relevant securities owned and/or controlled:

4,091,800

2.45

(2) Cash-settled derivatives:

0

(3) Stock-settled derivatives (including options) and agreements to purchase/sell:

0

     TOTAL:

4,091,800

2.45

All interests and all short positions should be disclosed.

Details of any open stock-settled derivative positions (including traded options), or agreements to purchase or sell relevant securities, should be given on a Supplemental Form 8 (Open Positions).

(b)        Rights to subscribe for new securities (including directors' and other employee options)

  • Class of relevant security in relation to which subscription right exists:

  • Details, including nature of the rights concerned and relevant percentages:

3.         DEALINGS (IF ANY) BY THE PERSON MAKING THE DISCLOSURE

Where there have been dealings in more than one class of relevant securities of the offeror or offeree named in 1(c), copy table 3(a), (b), (c) or (d) (as appropriate) for each additional class of relevant security dealt in.

The currency of all prices and other monetary amounts should be stated.

(a)        Purchases and sales

Class of relevant security

Common

Purchase/sale

Purchase

Number of securities

192,100

Price per unit

CAD 23.221

(b)        Cash-settled derivative transactions

  • Class of relevant security

  • Product description

    e.g. CFD

  • Nature of dealing

    e.g. opening/closing a long/short position, increasing/reducing a long/short position

  • Number of reference securities

  • Price per unit

(c)        Stock-settled derivative transactions (including options)

(i)         Writing, selling, purchasing or varying

  • Class of relevant security

  • Product description e.g. call option

  • Writing, purchasing, selling, varying etc.

  • Number of securities to which option relates

  • Exercise price per unit

  • Type

    e.g. American, European etc.

  • Expiry date

  • Option money paid/ received per unit

(ii)        Exercise

  • Class of relevant security

  • Product description

    e.g. call option

  • Exercising/ exercised against

  • Number of securities

  • Exercise price per unit

(d)        Other dealings (including subscribing for new securities)

  • Class of relevant security

  • Nature of dealing

    e.g. subscription, conversion

  • Details

  • Price per unit (if applicable)

4.         OTHER INFORMATION

(a)        Indemnity and other dealing arrangements

  • Details of any indemnity or option arrangement, or any agreement or understanding, formal or informal, relating to relevant securities which may be an inducement to deal or refrain from dealing entered into by the person making the disclosure and any party to the offer or any person acting in concert with a party to the offer:

    Irrevocable commitments and letters of intent should not be included.  If there are no such agreements, arrangements or understandings, state "none"

  • none

(b)        Agreements, arrangements or understandings relating to options or derivatives

  • Details of any agreement, arrangement or understanding, formal or informal, between the person making the disclosure and any other person relating to:

    (i)  the voting rights of any relevant securities under any option; or

    (ii) the voting rights or future acquisition or disposal of any relevant securities to which any derivative is referenced:

    If there are no such agreements, arrangements or understandings, state "none"

  • none

(c)        Attachments

  • Is a Supplemental Form 8 (Open Positions) attached?

  • NO

Date of disclosure:

25 July 2025

Contact name:

Federico Daneri

Telephone number*:

+39 02 85672200

Public disclosures under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a Regulatory Information Service.

The Panel's Market Surveillance Unit is available for consultation in relation to the Code's disclosure requirements on +44 (0)20 7638 0129.

*If the discloser is a natural person, a telephone number does not need to be included, provided contact information has been provided to the Panel's Market Surveillance Unit.

The Code can be viewed on the Panel's website at www.thetakeoverpanel.org.uk.
